name: Hanging Gardens
description: The Hanging Gardens of Babylon were one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Described as a remarkable feat of engineering with an ascending series of tiered gardens, containing a wide variety of trees, shrubs, and vines, resembling a large green mountain constructed of mud bricks. It is said to have been built by King Nebuchadnezzar II around 600 BC, as a gift to his Median wife, Queen Amytis, who longed for the green hills and valleys of her homeland. The gardens were reportedly built near the Euphrates River and relied on an elaborate irrigation system to bring water up to the highest levels, allowing the plants to thrive in the arid Babylonian climate. The existence of the Hanging Gardens is debated by historians, with some believing it to be a purely mythical creation or possibly confused with similar gardens in Nineveh. Despite the lack of definitive archaeological evidence, the Hanging Gardens have captured the imagination for centuries, symbolizing human ingenuity, love, and the power of nature to transform even the most barren landscapes. The gardens represent the peak of Babylonian power and architectural achievement, a testament to the empire's wealth and engineering prowess. They also embody the personal love and devotion of a king for his queen, creating a paradise in a foreign land.
subtitle: A verdant paradise in the desert.

keyMyths.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Gift
keyMyths.description: King Nebuchadnezzar II built the Hanging Gardens for his wife, Queen Amytis, who missed the green landscapes of her homeland, Media. It was an attempt to recreate the familiar beauty of her home in the flat, arid plains of Babylon.

keyMyths.title: The Irrigation System
keyMyths.description: The gardens were irrigated by a complex system of pumps and water wheels, drawing water from the Euphrates River to the highest terraces. This allowed for a diverse range of plant life to flourish in the seemingly inhospitable environment.

sources.author: Diodorus Siculus
sources.description: A universal history that describes the Hanging Gardens.
sources.text: A universal history that describes the Hanging Gardens.

sources.author: Strabo
sources.description: A geographical encyclopedia that mentions the Hanging Gardens as part of Babylon.
sources.text: A geographical encyclopedia that mentions the Hanging Gardens as part of Babylon.

sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
sources.work: The Mystery of the Hanging Garden of Babylon
sources.description: A modern scholarly work that reinterprets the location of the gardens.
